parties: Between Regina, Respondent, and Wing Hong To, Appellant
citation: [1992] B.C.J. No. 1700 DRS 93-04443 Vancouver Registry: CA012992 Also reported at 16 B.C.A.C. 223
court: British Columbia Court of Appeal McEachern, C.J.B.C., Proudfoot and Hollinrake JJ.A.
body_html:
Heard: May 20, 1992
Judgment: filed July 30, 1992
(18 pp.)

Criminal law -- Narcotic offences -- Heroin -- Possession for purposes of trafficking -- Inferences of knowledge -- Accused found in possession of bag containing large quantity of narcotics -- Reasonable to infer that accused knew bag containing a prohibited drug from circumstances of case.

This was an appeal from a conviction for possession of narcotics for purposes of trafficking. The accused was observed by police leaving the apartment in which he was staying with two plastic shopping bags which he placed in a car. A large amount of heroin was found in one of the bags. The trial judge did not believe the accused, who claimed another person told him to bring the bags and the car to him and inferred possession for trafficking purposes from the amount and value of the drugs.

HELD: The appeal was dismissed. The trial judge erred in inferring guilt merely from disbelieving the evidence of the accused without considering the consciousness of guilt. Having disbelieved the evidence of the accused, the trial judge was required to consider whether it would be rational, beyond a reasonable doubt, to infer knowledge from the remaining evidence including the facts of the case such as the unstamped passport of the accused on a trip to Hong Kong. Here it was legitimate to infer knowledge from the physical possession given the suspicious circumstances. The accused failed to offer any explanation to displace the inference and give rise to reasonable doubt.

STATUTES, REGULATIONS AND RULES CITED:

Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 686(1)(b)(iii).

Counsel for the Appellant: Terrence L. Robertson, Q.C.
Counsel for the Respondent: S.D. Frankel, Q.C.

McEACHERN C.J.B.C. (for the Court, dismissing the appeal):-- The facts in this case are not seriously in dispute, the real question being to determine whether it may legitimately be inferred that the accused had knowledge that a plastic bag he had in his physical possession contained a large quantity of heroin.

The accused was born in Hong Kong in 1966 and came to Canada in 1983. He settled first in Toronto, then moved to Calgary in 1987, and then to Vancouver in 1989 where he took up residence in a North Vancouver apartment owned by a friend, Lisa Ng.

A month after moving to Vancouver, the accused went to Hong Kong, but his Passport does not show any immigration stamps for this trip. When he returned to Vancouver on September 30, 1989 he listed the North Vancouver apartment as his address on a Customs Declaration.

When the accused returned to Vancouver, however, he went to a North Vancouver hotel where he registered under his former Toronto address. He said he stayed at the hotel because he knew some other persons were staying at the apartment while he was away.

On October 2, 1989 the accused left the hotel and went to the Ng apartment.

Apparently the accused had the use of a motor vehicle owned by the husband of Lisa Ng. He gained access to this vehicle by a combination rather than a key. He said the key to the apartment was in the vehicle. He stayed in the apartment several days.

On October 5, 1989 the accused was observed by police walking from the apartment to the vehicle. He was carrying two plastic shopping bags. The officers saw him open the driver's door and place the bags in the back seat. He was immediately arrested. In one shopping bag were some rented videos belonging to a store in Chinatown. In the other bag was another knotted plastic bag which was found to contain 21 packages comprising a total of 4.4 pounds of 96% pure heroin worth an alleged $1.35 million wholesale, and $11.95 million street value. There were no fingerprints on the inside bag.

The explanation given by the accused at trial was that on the day of his arrest he received a telephone call from a man known to him as Big Head Chee who was a friend of Lisa Ng and her husband. Chee told the accused he wished to use the vehicle, and he asked the accused to take a plastic bag, which he said was in a cupboard beside the washing machine, to the vehicle, and to leave the keys in the ignition. The accused said he did this without looking inside the bag and that he did not know it contained heroin or any other drug. He said he placed the videos in the car to remind himself to return them to the owner the next time he used the vehicle.

The accused called no other evidence.

The accused was tried by a judge without a jury. He found the accused guilty of possession for the purposes of trafficking, and sentenced him to eight years imprisonment. The oral Reasons for Judgment are brief. I shall quote some of them, and paraphrase other parts. They begin as follows:

I find the accused guilty. I do not believe his story, simply because it is too incredible.

The trial judge then briefly reviewed some of the facts and continued:

The defence of the accused is that he did not know he was carrying heroin. To conclude that the accused had knowledge of what he was carrying, I must look at the surrounding circumstances and draw an inference from those circumstances. The accused has given an explanation for the circumstances which, if capable of being true, provides a defence, however, I find that his explanation is not true and the only rational conclusion I can reach is that the accused knew that he was carrying heroin. I come to that conclusion for the following reasons: The accused presented as evidence at a bail hearing a Hong Kong passport. The last immigration stamps in the passport were dated 1983. He clearly had been to Hong Kong since that time. There were no stamps showing his entry to Hong Kong or to Canada in 1989. His explanation was that the stamps must have been put on a visa paper which was in the passport. That visa paper was never produced and the accused has no explanation for its disappearance.

It will be seen that the trial judge embarked upon an explanation for his "conclusion" that knowledge on the part of the accused of heroin in the plastic bag was the only "rational conclusion." The trial judge then dealt with the implausibility of the reasons the accused gave for staying in a hotel, of how he got the keys to the car, and of the explanation of the accused for taking the bag containing heroin to the car. Then the trial judge continued:

I find it unbelievable that a drug trafficker would leave 1.3 million dollars worth of heroin in an apartment unattended, then ask a virtual stranger to transport them to a car and leave them there so they can be picked up later. The trafficker would have to assume that To would not look into the bag and that To would do exactly as he was told. This all seems incredible to me, considering that To had only met Big Head once two or three months earlier. Mr. Zipp suggested that the explanation is that Big Head was relying on the innocence and naivete of To. I do not think that makes sense. Big Head had only met him once two or three months earlier. How could he know anything about To's personality.

The trial judge then disposed of the evidence of the accused relating to the video tapes, and said that was a very unlikely story. He found it "not believable" that the accused would get instructions to put the plastic bag into the car, and then decide to put the videos in the car because he had no certainty when he would see the car again.

The trial judge also discounted, as "impossible to believe" the evidence that the accused would fail to contact his friends while he was in jail awaiting trial simply because he saw no reason to contact them. He also commented skeptically about the evidence of the accused that he used his old Toronto address because he said he had an identification card with that address on it.

The sparse Reasons for Judgment, which comprise only five double spaced pages, conclude as follows:

No single factor is determinative, however, looking at the whole of his story, I find that it is not capable of being believed. I have no doubt that the accused knew that he was carrying heroin when he put the black plastic bag into the car. Taking into account the amount and value of the drugs, I have no difficulty concluding that he was in possession of them for the purpose of trafficking.

Mr. Robertson did not dispute the Crown's submission, on the authority of R. v. Blondin (1970), 2 C.C.C. (2d) 118 (B.C.C.A.), aff'd 4 C.C.C. (2d) 566n. (S.C.C.) and R. v. Saunders (1990), 56 C.C.C. 220 (S.C.C.), that the Crown is not required to establish that the accused knew the bag contained heroin. It is sufficient that the Crown prove the accused knew the bag contained a prohibited drug.

It is also common ground that the Crown must establish, beyond reasonable doubt that the accused had actual, physical control over the bag, which is not disputed, and that he knew the bag contained a prohibited drug: R. v. Beaver (1957), 118 C.C.C. 129 (S.C.C.).

It is apparent that the learned trial judge, having disbelieved all the evidence of the accused, inferred that the accused knew that the plastic bag contained a prohibited drug.

The first question is whether the evidence permitted the learned trial judge to conclude beyond a reasonable doubt that knowledge on the part of the accused was the only reasonable inference to be drawn from the proven facts: R. v. Cooper (1977), 34 C.C.C. (2d) 18 (S.C.C.) per Ritchie J. at p. 33.

In this respect, our function is not to second-guess the trial judge, but merely to examine the evidence, and consider whether the judge has correctly applied the law to the facts of the case. This process was partly described by McIntyre J. speaking for the Court in R. v. Yebes (1987), 36 C.C.C. (3d) 417, at p. 430:

...in order to apply the test the court must re-examine and to some extent reweigh and consider the effect of the evidence. This process will be the same whether the case is based on circumstantial or direct evidence.

Mr. Robertson relies upon R. v. Hess (1948), 94 C.C.C. 48 (B.C.C.A) where this Court, by a majority, allowed an appeal against a conviction in a drug case where, as here, the question was whether knowledge could be inferred from circumstantial evidence. The facts in that case, however, although suspicious, were consistent with the accused being a casual finder of the drugs which had been left lying at the base of a signpost at a city intersection. The police apprehended the accused before he had an opportunity to open the package, or otherwise demonstrate that he knew what was in it.

What is troubling about this case is the fact that a fair reading of the Reasons for Judgment indicates that the learned trial judge found the accused guilty because he disbelieved the evidence given by the accused. It is true that the judge said:

To conclude that the accused had knowledge of what he was carrying, I must look at the surrounding circumstances and draw an inference from those circumstances.

The balance of the Reasons for Judgment, however, are devoted entirely to restating and rejecting the evidence of the accused. In my judgment, it is not jurisprudentially correct to infer guilt merely from disbelieving the evidence of the accused: see R. v. Levy (1990), 62 C.C.C. (3d) 97, (Ont. C.A.), per Doherty J.A. at p. 101 where that learned judge said:

Rejection of evidence given by an accused cannot become positive evidence of the accused's guilt. However, a statement by an accused which is proved to be false may permit inferences that the accused lied because he has a guilty conscience and that he has a guilty conscience because he committed the offence alleged: R. v. Davison, DeRosie and MacArthur (1947), 20 C.C.C. (2d) 424 at pp. 428-30 6 O.R. 103 (C.A.); leave to appeal refused C.C.C. loc. cit. 1947 S.C.R. viii...

(my emphasis)

It does not appear that the learned trial judge decided this case on the basis of consciousness of guilt. In any event, consciousness of guilt cannot be inferred just because the evidence of the accused is disbelieved. It can, however, be inferred from proven falsehoods, but even then, it is necessary to consider why the accused lied, for there are many reasons for accused persons to lie which are not necessarily indicative of guilt. In this respect, see R. v. Davison, DeRosie & McArthur (1974), 20 C.C.C. (2d) 424 (Ont. C.A.). In this case, none of the statements made by the accused were shown to be untrue. They were simply disbelieved.

Thus it seems to me, the trial judge, having disbelieved the evidence of the accused, was required to consider whether it would be rational, beyond reasonable doubt, to infer knowledge from the balance of the evidence, including, in this case, facts such as the unstamped passport of the accused which was adduced into evidence during the defence evidence.

There are many authorities where Courts have considered the difference between legitimate inference and conjecture or speculation. O'Halloran J.A. in Hess referred in this respect to the discussion of this question in the context of a civil case by Lord Wright in Caswell v. Powell Duffryn Associated Collieries Ltd., [1940] A.C. 152, but then went on to say:

But even if in a civil case these inculpatory inferences could be drawn (which I do not concede) equally with the exculpatory inferences, nevertheless in a criminal case, under the principle in Hodge's Case (1838), 2 Lewin 227, 168 E.R. 1136, the objective facts must point to the inculpatory inferences conclusively and be incapable of any rational inferences excluding guilt.

Hodge's Case, of course, has been replaced by Cooper as the authoritative statement of the law in this context but the principle remains the same.

O'Halloran J.A. was not prepared to infer guilt in Hess because, with the presumption of innocence in mind, the evidence, although suspicious, was consistent with the accused being an innocent finder of the package of drugs. On the narrow facts of that case, the inferences pointing to guilt were more or less equally balanced with inferences pointing towards innocence. Evidence crystallizing suspicion into legal proof was absent. Thus the case was not proven beyond reasonable doubt.

In Caswell v. Powell Duffryn Associated Collieries Ltd., which was a civil case, Lord Wright at p. 169 said:

Inference must be carefully distinguished from conjecture or speculation. There can be no inference unless there are objective facts from which to infer the other facts which it is sought to establish. In some cases the other facts can be inferred with as much practical certainty as it they had been actually observed. In other cases the inference does not go beyond reasonable probability.

This Court has struggled with this problem in many cases. R. v. Spooner (1954), 109 C.C.C. 57 (B.C.C.A.) is another example of the principle stated in Hess. The accused, an interdicted person, was sitting in a car with several others and, on request, she passed a soft drink bottle which was later found to contain liquor from a person in the front seat to another person in the back seat. She was charged and convicted of being found "in possession of liquor" contrary to relevant liquor legislation. She was acquitted without reasons for judgment by a County Court Judge, and the Crown brought an appeal to this Court on a point of law.

The accused gave evidence saying she did not know the bottle contained liquor.

Sloan C.J.B.C. and Bird J.A. dismissed the Crown appeal on the ground that mens rea was an essential part of the Crown's case and that it had not been shown on the simple facts of the case.

O'Halloran J.A. concurred, but delivered a separate judgment in which he discussed the problem this appeal raises. At p. 63 that learned judge said:

Proof of mere possession in some instances may be sufficient at the end of the Crown case for conviction [under the relevant statute]; that is to say, at that stage mere possession may in itself obviously imply knowledge of the thing possessed in the absence of lawful possession appearing in the evidence of the Crown witnesses or in their cross-examination. So at the close of the Crown case proving mere possession, if the accused fails to call evidence, the Crown may have made out a case warranting conviction...(my emphasis)

For the purposes of his judgment O'Halloran J.A. treated the defences of mens rea and lack of knowledge as equivalents, and he went on to point out that when the Crown's case establishes physical possession, it may become incumbent upon the accused to set up a defence of lack of mens rea or some other defence such as lack of knowledge of what is physically possessed. He also pointed out that the degree of onus of proof of lack of mens rea (or knowledge) then resting upon the accused is not one pointing to conclusiveness beyond reasonable doubt, but is sufficient if the balance of probabilities lies in his favour. This is often described as an onus not of proof, but it is really an onus of adducing evidence without which it may be that the Crown has proven its case beyond reasonable doubt.

O'Halloran J.A. summarized the case at p. 64 as follows:

In short, it was open to the [accused] "to prove" that she lacked mens rea, viz., that as an interdict she did not have possession of liquor, or did not know that what she had was liquor... (my emphasis)

In this case it seems that if the defence had called no evidence whatever, the learned Judge would have been compelled judicially on the Crown evidence to have inferred possession of liquor and mens rea and convict [the accused]. But having heard her evidence referred to above the learned Judge could judicially have found as he did, that she had rebutted possession and the presence of mens rea to the degree of preponderance of probabilities in her favour...

This Court has applied this judicial reasoning in many cases. In R. v. Guiney (1961) the accused picked up a cache of drugs in a lane. Upon hearing approaching police officers he dropped the cache and started to walk away. He first said he went into the lane to relieve himself, but he later said he learned of the cache by overhearing a conversation in a beer parlour, and believed the cache contained money or jewellery. He was disbelieved by the trial judge. Because of this, Sheppard J.A., for the Court, said at p. 409:

...hence there is no explanation to rebut the inference from his actions and the attempted secrecy of his movements that he knew the contents.

In this respect, see also R. v. MacFadden (1981), 60 C.C.C. (2d) 305 (B.C.C.A.); R. v. Stephen, unreported, decided March 7, 1973 (B.C.C.A.); R. v. Welter, CA006144, February 26, 1988, (B.C.C.A). In each of these cases the Court drew inferences amounting to guilt because the highly suspicious facts permitted no other rational conclusion, and the accused either did not give evidence or was not believed so the rational inference was not rebutted.

Thus it seems to me, with respect, that it is legitimate to infer knowledge from mere physical possession in proper circumstances which inference will be displaced if an explanation is offered which raises a reasonable doubt or if, as in Hess, other inferences consistent with innocence may be drawn from all the proven circumstances. It is this rational process which distinguishes inference from speculation or conjecture. At the end of the case, of course, the Crown must prove the guilt of the accused beyond a reasonable doubt, but once that was done, on the circumstances of this case, the burden of adducing an explanation that raises a reasonable doubt, or of extracting reasonable doubt from the evidence, rested upon the accused.

The total rejection of the evidence of the accused is tantamount to the accused having given no evidence or offered no explanation, and it then became necessary to consider whether, on all of the evidence, an inference of knowledge may be drawn from the proven facts, or whether, in all the circumstances, a fair consideration of the evidence either leaves the scales in even balance, or tilts one way or the other. The answers to those questions, of course, indicate whether the Crown's case has been proven to the requisite degree, that is beyond a reasonable doubt.

I have reached the conclusion that it would be legitimate, on the broad facts of this case, to infer beyond a reasonable doubt that the accused knew the plastic bag contained a prohibited drug. In fact, I think that would be the only reasonable inference which could be drawn. I believe that not just because the accused was found in physical control of the drugs, but also because of the highly suspicious circumstances and the immense quantity and value of the drugs. These facts make it so unlikely that such a large quantity of drugs would be entrusted to anyone who did not know what was in the bags that such a possibility may safely be rejected. In addition, his antecedent, highly suspicious conduct, most of which was proven affirmatively before he gave his disbelieved evidence, make it impossible to draw any inferences which would displace the legitimate inference which may be drawn from physical possession that he knew what he was possessing.

It must be remembered that we are not expected to treat real life cases as a completely intellectual exercise where no conclusion can be reached if there is the slightest competing possibility. The criminal law requires a very high degree of proof, especially for inferences consistent with guilt, but it does not demand certainty. I do not think it could properly be said that an inference of knowledge in this case would be unreasonable or unsupported by the evidence.

The question which remains to be answered, however, is whether the apparent reliance placed by the learned trial judge upon the fact that the evidence of the accused was disbelieved which I consider to be legal error, makes it necessary to set the conviction aside. This of course raises the question of Code s. 686. (1) (b) (iii).

Under this sub-section the Court may, notwithstanding its opinion that the appeal might be decided in favour of the appellant, nevertheless dismiss the appeal if it is of the opinion that no substantial wrong or miscarriage of justice has occurred: Colpitts v. The Queen, [1966] 1 C.C.C. 146 (S.C.C.).

There is a substantial body of jurisprudence governing the construction to be placed upon this section. Generally speaking, the Court will not give effect to it unless the Crown establishes that the result would necessarily have been the same if the error had not occurred.

In my judgment the legal error in this case made no difference because the highly suspicious circumstances and the absence of a believable or any explanation make an inference of knowledge the only rational alternative. It would be otherwise if a competing inference consistent with the absence of knowledge could be drawn from the evidence, but no such inference arises. In fact, as I have already said, an inference of knowledge was the only rational inference which the learned trial judge could have drawn from the evidence.

I would accordingly apply Code s. 686. (1) (b) (iii), and dismiss this appeal accordingly.

McEACHERN C.J.B.C.

PROUDFOOT J.A.:-- I agree.

HOLLINRAKE J.A.:-- I agree.
